# Elliot & Aarav's COMP110 Website

## Introduction

Our goal was to identify the needs within this course and propose a plausable solution. Specifically, we wanted to address the learning curve for pure beginners. In this project, we analyzed the survey data to test a specific hypothesis: Would offering an optional, slower-paced introductory section of COMP 110 benefit students who have absolutely no prior coding experience? Here is our thought process, data visualizations, and final recommendations based on what the numbers actually told us.

## Data

<img width="553" height="287" alt="image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/b7545740-5a00-46e2-9239-5128cbcf452b" />

<img width="550" height="287" alt="image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/a7cff5cc-5c97-4253-9a1d-18720d22c7f2" />


<img width="554" height="292" alt="image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/756b8d27-9919-49c1-b557-a5e4ef90a00a" />


## Analysis & Conclusion
Based on our analysis of the survey data, the numbers clearly support the implementation of an optional, slower-paced introductory track for COMP 110. The visualizations demonstrate a stark contrast in the student experience: absolute beginners (those with less than one month of prior experience) found the course significantly more difficult, reported that the pace of the lectures moved too quickly, and relied much more heavily on 1-on-1 office hours compared to their more experienced peers. To alleviate this bottleneck and reduce beginner burnout, our primary recommendation is to offer a dedicated section tailored to this demographic. Alternatively, if splitting the lecture is not feasible, the department could explore offering a 1-credit corequisite lab session focused entirely on foundational review and guided practice.
